original_hypothesis.title: Auction Permanent Fund Lock via Malicious Highest Bidder Contract Reverting onERC721Received
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: A malicious contract bids to become the highest bidder, then its onERC721Received callback reverts. claimAuction always reverts on safeTransferFrom, permanently locking ALL bidder ETH in the contract. There is no alternative refund path since cancelBid requires block.timestamp <= auctionEndTime, and auctionDemo has no emergencyWithdraw function.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: safeTransferFrom to a reverting receiver prevents the entire claimAuction transaction from completing. The auctionDemo contract lacks any fund recovery mechanism. All losing bidder funds and the NFT become permanently inaccessible.

proof_of_signal.pattern: IERC721(gencore).safeTransferFrom(ownerOfToken, highestBidder, _tokenid) called inside claimAuction loop; if highestBidder is a contract that reverts in onERC721Received, entire transaction reverts. No alternative code path for refunds. No emergencyWithdraw exists in auctionDemo.

preconditions: An active auction exists (mintAndAuction was called)
Attacker deploys a contract that implements onERC721Received to always revert
Attacker's contract places the highest bid via participateToAuction
Auction end time has passed (block.timestamp >= auctionEndTime)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: safeTransferFrom triggers onERC721Received callback on the receiver before returning
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker contract reverts in onERC721Received, causing claimAuction to always revert. cancelBid requires timestamp <= endTime so it becomes unusable after the auction ends.
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack: all losing bidder funds permanently locked. Attacker also loses their bid but the victim impact is multiplied.

economic_questions: Is there any admin override that can bypass safeTransferFrom to the malicious contract?
Can the gencore address be updated in auctionDemo to route around the revert?
What is the maximum TVL at risk in a single auction?
validation_requirements: Deploy malicious bidder contract, win auction, confirm claimAuction reverts permanently
Verify no fund recovery mechanism exists in auctionDemo

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_execution_cost: ~0.01 ETH gas for deploying contract and placing bid
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Zero direct profit; griefing locks ALL other bidders' ETH permanently
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Single transaction to bid; permanent lock after auction end

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Capital equal to highest bid amount
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Bid as late as possible to minimize capital at risk
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: All auction participants permanently lose funds; protocol reputation destroyed

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All losing bidders for the targeted auction
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Sum of all active bids in the auction

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: gencore is immutable in auctionDemo constructor - no admin update function exists
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm safeTransferFrom revert propagates through entire claimAuction call

validation_result.mechanism: DoS via reverting onERC721Received callback in claimAuction
validation_result.impact_summary: A malicious contract winning an auction permanently locks all bidder ETH and the NFT by reverting in onERC721Received, as there is no alternative refund path or emergency withdrawal in auctionDemo.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The vulnerability is a Denial of Service attack on the auction settlement process. The claimAuction function uses IERC721.safeTransferFrom to transfer the NFT to the highest bidder. safeTransferFrom invokes the onERC721Received callback on the recipient if it is a contract. If the highest bidder is a malicious contract that reverts in onERC721Received, the entire claimAuction transaction reverts. After the auction ends (block.timestamp > auctionEndTime), cancelBid and cancelAllBids are both blocked by their require(block.timestamp <= minter.getAuctionEndTime(_tokenid)) check. The auctionDemo contract contains no emergencyWithdraw function, no admin override for fund recovery, and no alternative claim path. This means ALL ETH deposited by ALL bidders (winning and losing) is permanently locked in the contract with no recovery mechanism.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The safeTransferFrom call in claimAuction sends the NFT to highestBidder, triggering onERC721Received. If this reverts, the entire transaction reverts because there is no try/catch. The auctionClaim[_tokenid] = true is set before the loop but is rolled back on revert. cancelBid requires block.timestamp <= auctionEndTime which fails post-auction. No emergencyWithdraw exists in AuctionDemo.sol. The vulnerability path is fully confirmed in the source code.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: IERC721(gencore).safeTransferFrom(ownerOfToken, highestBidder, _tokenid) triggers onERC721Received on highestBidder. If highestBidder is a contract that reverts in onERC721Received, the entire claimAuction transaction reverts. Since the revert happens before any state is committed (auctionClaim is rolled back), claimAuction can never succeed. This permanently locks all bidder funds.
cancelBid requires block.timestamp <= auctionEndTime. After the auction ends, this function is permanently blocked for all bidders, eliminating the only alternative fund recovery path.
Same timestamp restriction as cancelBid - unusable after auction ends.

calling_sequence: 1. Attacker deploys MaliciousBidder contract with onERC721Received that always reverts
2. MaliciousBidder.bid() calls participateToAuction(_tokenid) with highest bid amount
3. participateToAuction succeeds: msg.value > returnHighestBid, timestamp <= endTime, auctionStatus == true
4. auctionInfoData[_tokenid].push(newBid) stores the malicious bidder's bid
9. auctionClaim[_tokenid] = true (tentatively set, will be rolled back on revert)
10. Loop finds highest bidder entry, calls IERC721(gencore).safeTransferFrom(owner, maliciousBidder, tokenid)
11. safeTransferFrom calls onERC721Received on maliciousBidder → REVERTS
12. Entire claimAuction transaction reverts, auctionClaim is reset to false
13. cancelBid/cancelAllBids revert with 'Auction ended' since timestamp > endTime
14. No emergencyWithdraw exists in auctionDemo - funds permanently locked
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The critical path is: claimAuction → safeTransferFrom(ownerOfToken, highestBidder, _tokenid) → ERC721._checkOnERC721Received → MaliciousBidder.onERC721Received() → revert. The revert propagates up through safeTransferFrom, through the for loop, and out of claimAuction. No try/catch wraps the safeTransferFrom call. After the auction end time passes, cancelBid's require(block.timestamp <= minter.getAuctionEndTime(_tokenid)) permanently blocks the only refund path. The auctionDemo contract has exactly 8 functions: constructor, participateToAuction, returnHighestBid, returnHighestBidder, claimAuction, cancelBid, cancelAllBids, returnBids - none of which provide an emergency fund recovery mechanism.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: The deployment script (fixturesDeployment.js) does not deploy the auctionDemo contract, but the vulnerability is inherent in the contract code regardless of deployment configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is fully valid and requires minimal capital and sophistication: (1) Deploy a contract with `function onERC721Received(address,address,uint256,bytes) external pure returns(bytes4) { revert(); }` (2) From this contract, call participateToAuction with the highest bid (3) Wait for auction to end (4) claimAuction will always revert. The WinnerOrAdminRequired modifier allows admins to call claimAuction, but this doesn't help because the safeTransferFrom to the malicious highestBidder reverts regardless of who initiates the call. There is no code path in the contract that can extract the locked funds.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL: All ETH from all bidders in the targeted auction is permanently locked. The NFT remains with the original owner but cannot be re-auctioned through this contract for the same tokenId (auctionClaim stays false but claimAuction always reverts, and there's no reset mechanism). The attacker sacrifices their own bid amount but causes total loss for all other bidders. For a high-value auction with many participants, the damage multiplier is significant.
recommended_mitigations: Use transferFrom instead of safeTransferFrom to avoid the onERC721Received callback, or wrap safeTransferFrom in a try/catch
Separate the NFT transfer and refund logic into distinct functions so refunds can proceed independently of the NFT transfer
Add an emergencyWithdraw function gated by admin access to the auctionDemo contract
Implement a pull-based refund pattern where losing bidders can withdraw their funds independently via a separate function after the auction ends
Add a fallback mechanism that uses transferFrom if safeTransferFrom fails
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy NextGenAdmins, NextGenCore, NextGenMinterContract, auctionDemo. 2. Create collection, set data, set costs, set phases, add randomizer. 3. Call mintAndAuction to create an auctioned token, approve auctionDemo for the token. 4. Deploy MaliciousBidder contract with reverting onERC721Received. 5. Have legitimate user1 bid 1 ETH via participateToAuction. 6. Have MaliciousBidder bid 2 ETH via participateToAuction. 7. Advance time past auctionEndTime. 8. Call claimAuction from admin - expect revert. 9. Call cancelBid from user1 - expect revert with 'Auction ended'. 10. Verify contract balance = 3 ETH with no way to extract.
uncertainties: The exact line numbers in the proof_of_signal (104-120) could not be verified with absolute precision since the source doesn't have line numbers, but the pattern and function are confirmed present
additional_testing_needed: Verify that the gencore ERC721 implementation uses _checkOnERC721Received in safeTransferFrom (standard OZ behavior, expected to be present)
Test with a contract that consumes all gas in onERC721Received vs one that simply reverts
Confirm that no governance upgrade path exists that could rescue funds (e.g., updating the gencore address in auctionDemo - which is not possible as gencore is immutable in the constructor)
